smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: 3 ads for Barrelhand
thread.body_text:
hey Karel, spent a couple minutes on the Barrelhand site and honestly can't figure out why we're not already working together haha.

anyway, that's not why I'm writing. I run Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of pitching you some BS, I actually built you a few. (gotta provide value before anything)

built 3 ad mockups from Barrelhand's real products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and reviews. it's done and sitting ready on my end.

want me to send it over?

- Kyle

Hi Kyle, 

 We are currently running meta ads, would be curious what you had in mind and how you would approach it differently.
 

 -Karel

here you go Karel - 3 ad mockups built from Barrelhand's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Barrelhand sells one $9,750 spaceflight-grade mechanical watch to serious collectors, has no reviews and no live ads we can see, so this call is about starting clean with high-consideration ads that build trust, not chasing quick sales.

deep_dive.what_they_are: An engineering-first independent watchmaking brand out of San Francisco. They build ultra-premium mechanical toolwatches aimed at spaceflight and the collector frontier lane. Their whole story is optimism about the future and human space exploration.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Their flagship is the Monolith, a mechanical toolwatch priced at $9,750. It comes in three strap colorways: Barrelhand Orange, Black, and Black & Orange. Next batch deliveries start Q4 2026 and orders are fully refundable before shipment. They also have a Memory Disc initiative and something called Project One, plus downloadable non-commercial Monolith Source Files. Karel said in email they are already running Meta ads, even though we could not find any live.
deep_dive.positioning: Top-tier, technical, mission-driven. Not a heritage luxury house. They frame themselves as builders of tools for life beyond Earth, engineering over prestige.
deep_dive.who_buys: Serious watch collectors and space-and-tech enthusiasts with high disposable income. People who value engineering proof, being early to a frontier story, and products built to last and be serviced over time.
strengths: Strong, clear story and voice. Frontier and engineering language gives us real ad angles that most watch brands cannot copy.
Very high price point means even a handful of sales is meaningful revenue, so we do not need huge volume to matter.
gaps: No visible reviews or social proof anywhere on the site. At $9,750 that is a big trust problem for cold traffic.
We could not find any live Meta ads despite Karel saying they run them, so we do not actually know what they have tried or what worked.

game_plan.headline: Start from zero with trust-building ads for the Monolith, prove one angle can bring in buyers, then widen and add a second product.

phases.goal: Prove that paid ads can bring in qualified buyers for the Monolith at all. Learn which angle and which audience respond, without expecting scale.
campaigns: One cold prospecting campaign to new audiences, testing the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads against each other.
One simple retargeting campaign for people who visited the site or watched the ads but did not buy.
lead_products: The Monolith is the lead and really the only product to lead with. It is their flagship, it is the one with a price and colorways and a delivery date, and it is what the whole site is built around. We lead with the Barrelhand Orange colorway first because it is their signature color and the most distinctive in a feed.
phases.creative: Go live first with the Scroll-Stopper ad that leans hardest on the engineering and spaceflight story, because that is the brand's real edge and it filters for the collector who can afford this. At a $9,750 price the job of the ad is not to sell on the spot, it is to stop the right person and pull them into the story. Point traffic to the Monolith page and the newsletter for early access, since that low-commitment step fits a long buying decision.
phases.budget: See estimates below. We start small on purpose. This is a learning month, not a scaling month.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Not a pile of sales. Success is clear signal: one or two ads getting cheap clicks and quality site visits, email signups coming in, and ideally one or two serious buyers or deposit-style orders. We want to know which angle and audience to put money behind in month two.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and widen the audience around it.
campaigns: Scale the winning cold ad and audience, and cut the losers.
Widen to new lookalike and interest audiences built off month-one visitors and any buyers.
Strengthen retargeting with a trust-focused ad, once we have real customer proof to show.
lead_products: Still the Monolith. We now test the other strap colorways (Black, Black & Orange) as fresh creative to keep the ads from going stale to the same people.
phases.creative: Take the winning angle from month one and make two or three new versions of it. Bring in any social proof we have gathered by now (owner stories, press, founder credibility) because trust is the biggest blocker at this price. Keep the newsletter and early-access offer as the soft entry point.
phases.budget: Step spend up only on what is already working. We will size this once month one gives us real numbers.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad still performs as we spend more on it. Wider audiences bring buyers at a return the margin can carry. Email list is growing steadily as a pool of future buyers.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and add a second product to the mix.
campaigns: Scale the full winning structure across cold and retargeting.
Launch a second-product campaign built around the Memory Disc or Project One.
Ongoing retargeting that now has a real story: buyers, waitlist, delivery timeline.
lead_products: Monolith stays the main driver. We add the Memory Disc initiative as a second angle, because it is a lower-commitment way into the brand story and can catch people who love the mission but are not ready to spend $9,750 yet.
phases.creative: Fresh creative for the second product plus refreshed Monolith ads so the audience does not burn out. Lean into the Q4 2026 delivery timeline to create honest urgency (real deadline, not fake countdowns).
phases.budget: Scale in line with whatever return the first 60 days proved. We do not commit to a scale number now because we have no account data yet.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A repeatable setup that brings in Monolith buyers at a return their margin allows, plus a second product feeding new people into the brand and the email list.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: At this high a price the ad's job is to bring in the right visitor, not to make cheap sales. This gets us enough daily clicks to see which of the 3 ads and which audience the right buyer responds to, without spending big before we know anything.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We are not putting a revenue number on this yet
estimates.basis: With a $9,750 product, revenue swings hugely on just one or two sales, so any 90-day figure would be a guess. We would need their real conversion rate and how many Monoliths they actually sell in a normal month before we promise a number. That is a discovery question, not something we should say as fact.

assumptions: The Monolith at $9,750 is the main product and the one they most want to sell. If a different product is their real priority, the plan changes.
Their margin can carry paid ads at this price, and buying is a longer decision, not an impulse. If the sales cycle is very long or margin is thin, spend and expectations shift.
They can take orders now (or deposits/waitlist) even with Q4 2026 delivery. If they cannot actually sell until later, month-one goals shift to list building only.

questions.question: You mentioned you're already running Meta ads. Can you walk me through what's live right now and what you're spending?
questions.listening_for: We could not find any live ads, so this matters. Listen for whether they actually run ads now, spend a little and stop, or confused ads with boosted posts. This tells you if we are fixing something or starting from zero.

questions.question: Who's running those ads today, you or someone on your side?
questions.listening_for: In-house, an agency, or a freelancer. If there is an agency already, find out why they took our call. If it is the founder doing it themselves, they likely have no time and that is our opening.

questions.question: In a normal month, how many Monoliths do you sell, and through what channel?
questions.listening_for: This is the single most important number. At $9,750 even a few sales is real money. It also tells you if ads even make sense yet, or if they sell mostly by word of mouth or waitlist. Do not guess this yourself.

questions.question: On a $9,750 watch, roughly what does the margin allow you to spend to land one sale?
questions.listening_for: Whether they can afford to pay for buyers at all. High-price goods often have room here, but confirm it. If they have no idea, that is a flag they have never thought about paid acquisition properly.

questions.question: When someone lands on the Monolith page, what usually happens? Do they buy, join the list, or drop off?
questions.listening_for: Where it breaks. If people come but do not buy, the problem is trust or the page, not the ads. If nobody comes at all, it is a traffic problem we can fix.

questions.question: I noticed there are no reviews or customer stories on the site. Do you have any buyer feedback, press, or founder story we could use?
questions.listening_for: This is your biggest lever. At this price cold buyers need proof. If they have press, collector testimonials, or a strong founder story, we build ads around it. If they have nothing, month one has to build that.

questions.question: Can people order or put a deposit down now, even though deliveries start Q4 2026?
questions.listening_for: Whether we can even drive sales yet, or just build a waitlist. This changes what month one is for. Fully refundable before shipment is a strong thing to say in ads, so confirm it.

questions.question: What's the actual buying journey? Do people decide in a week, or is it months of emails and questions first?
questions.listening_for: A $9,750 watch is rarely an impulse buy. If it takes months, ads feed the top and email closes the sale, and we should not judge ads on week-one sales.

questions.question: Are you seeing people search for you by name, and do you show up when they search things like 'space watch' or 'toolwatch'?
questions.listening_for: An honest opening for Google. Collectors researching a $9,750 watch do search brand names and category terms. If people already look them up, search ads and SEO can catch buyers who are further along than cold Meta traffic. This is a real opening, not a forced one, so it is worth raising.

questions.question: Do you get press or blog write-ups in the watch and space world, and are they sending you traffic?
questions.listening_for: Watch collecting has strong media and forums. If write-ups exist, SEO and content can compound over time. Only push this if they say people find them through articles and search, not just social.

objections.objection: We already run Meta ads, why do we need you?
objections.answer: Fair question. When I looked I could not actually find your ads running, which usually means they are off, tiny, or not set up to keep going. That is the first thing I would want to see with you on the call. If they are working, great, I will tell you that. If they are not, I have already built you 3 ads and a full plan for free, so you can judge my thinking before you spend a dollar with me.

objections.objection: Our watch is $9,750, nobody buys something that expensive from a Facebook ad.
objections.answer: Agreed, and I would not run ads that try to close the sale in one click. At this price the ad's job is to stop the right collector and pull them into your story and your email list. The sale happens later, over emails and their own research. That is exactly why my month-one plan is about learning and list building, not chasing quick checkouts.

objections.objection: We have no reviews or social proof, won't that kill the ads?
objections.answer: It makes cold selling harder, which is why I flagged it. But your engineering and spaceflight story is real proof most watch brands do not have. Month one we lead with that and start gathering buyer stories and press we can show. By month two the ads carry that trust. We build the proof in on purpose.

objections.objection: Can you tell me what kind of return I'll get?
objections.answer: Not honestly, not yet. I do not have your sales numbers or conversion rate, so any number I gave you would be a guess, and I would rather lose the deal than make one up. What I can tell you is how I would spend to find the answer fast and cheap in the first 30 days, then size everything off what we actually see. If you share your real numbers on this call I can get a lot sharper.

red_flags.flag: No reviews or social proof anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: At $9,750 cold buyers need serious trust before they act. With no visible proof, early ads will struggle to convert until we build in the story, press, or buyer testimonials.

red_flags.flag: No live Meta ads found, but Karel says they run them.
red_flags.why_it_matters: There is a gap between what we can see and what they claim. We do not actually know what they have tried, so do not assume. Get the truth early on the call so the plan fits reality.

red_flags.flag: Very high price and a delivery date almost a year out (Q4 2026).
red_flags.why_it_matters: The buying decision is long and orders may just be deposits or a waitlist. Judging ads on quick sales would be wrong, and we should set that expectation up front so month one is not seen as a failure.

red_flags.flag: No scrapable catalog or pricing beyond the one product.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We are basing almost everything on the Monolith. If other products matter more to their business, the plan needs to change, so confirm the lineup before committing.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads: serious collectors researching a $9,750 watch tend to search brand names and terms like 'space watch' or 'toolwatch'. Catching people who are already looking is a real fit here, unlike a brand nobody searches for. Confirm search demand in discovery first.

SEO and content: watch collecting has active media, forums, and reviewers. If write-ups already send them traffic, content can compound over time and catch buyers deep in research. Only worth it if they confirm people find them through articles and search.
